confirms.fact: Leah (Lusia) Weitzner born in Bolechów on 8 April 1913
David Rapaport born in Nadwórna on 25 December 1911
David and Leah married in February 1938
Weitzner family emigrated to Palestine in 1933
David lived under false identity in Lwów during 1941
David was registered as Displaced Person in Brussels
Dov Bernard Rapaport born in Brussels in 1946
Rebeka Griffel (Regina Rapaport) born in Nadwórna in 1888

contradicts.evidence: Memoir indicates Shimon was born in June 1937 (page 82: 'Shimon was born in 1939' is contradicted by page 6 which lists his birth as 1937, and by the narrative that he was 2 years old when war broke out in 1939, making him born in 1937). The family data lists birth_year as 1937 which aligns with memoir narrative.

new_people_proposed.name: Pietrozycki
new_people_proposed.relationship: Good-hearted Gentile neighbor in Nadwórna; helped hide David and Lusia in forest while caring for Shimon

new_people_proposed.name: Ivanov
new_people_proposed.relationship: Russian Gentile neighbor and father of three in Nadwórna; helped arrange Shimon's transfer to Lwów; warned Lusia of planned action against children

new_people_proposed.name: Hormak family
new_people_proposed.relationship: Gentile dealers in Jewish property in Nadwórna and Lwów; helped Lusia with food, housing, and escape to Lwów; sister (Mrs. Inovska) sheltered Shimon in Lwów

new_people_proposed.name: Maria Ciccelik
new_people_proposed.relationship: Polish peasant woman from Nadwórna whose birth certificate Lusia used to forge identity documents; protected Shimon in Nadwórna from 1942-1945

new_people_proposed.name: Pesach
new_people_proposed.relationship: Friend and fellow worker of David; needed Lusia's help suturing himself to hide circumcision

new_people_proposed.name: Dr. Korner
new_people_proposed.relationship: Dentist from Landsberg; murdered by Germans for possessing gold; Lusia helped his 14-year-old daughter

new_people_proposed.name: Esther Weiss
new_people_proposed.relationship: Teacher who conducted interviews with Lusia in 1986 and produced original booklet 'Lusia'

new_people_proposed.name: Penina Hauser
new_people_proposed.relationship: Co-editor with Esther Weiss; helped produce the memoir 'The Story of Lusia'

new_people_proposed.name: Moshe Guter
new_people_proposed.relationship: Journalist editor who brought 'The Story of Lusia' to print

new_people_proposed.name: Donner
new_people_proposed.relationship: German manager of Maritzka Square commercial center in Lwów; promoted Lusia to manage art objects shop

new_people_proposed.name: Lota Rapaport
new_people_proposed.relationship: Lusia's sister-in-law (Memek's sister); lived in Lwów ghetto; bought forged US documents; disappeared (presumed killed)

new_people_proposed.name: Dr. Reuben Hackt
new_people_proposed.relationship: Swiss doctor met in Slovakia after war; urged Lusia to document her Holocaust experiences; later became well-known personality in Haifa (owner of Tenuva grain storage)

new_people_proposed.name: Tami Rapaport
new_people_proposed.relationship: Shimon's wife; married after the war

new_people_proposed.name: Akiva Alder
new_people_proposed.relationship: Son of Lusia's sister Zippora (Tzipora); political correspondent for Ha'aretz newspaper

new_places_proposed.name: Stry
new_places_proposed.context: City in Poland where Lusia attended gymnasium 'Piat-Lokus' (girls' school); about an hour from Bolechów

new_places_proposed.name: Zakopane
new_places_proposed.context: Mountain town in Poland where Polish peasants carve art objects; Lusia traveled there with German manager Donner

new_places_proposed.name: Cyprus
new_places_proposed.context: Location of British detention camps where Shimon and David were held for about a year after capture of the Theodor Herzl ship

new_places_proposed.name: Atlit
new_places_proposed.context: Detention camp in Mandatory Palestine where Shimon was transferred from Cyprus; described as 'the corridor to freedom'

new_places_proposed.name: Sharon region (Israel)
new_places_proposed.context: Region in Palestine/Israel where Lusia traveled with her father; known for citrus orchards

new_places_proposed.name: Tirat HaCarmel
new_places_proposed.context: Location of school in Israel where Lusia did volunteer work with B'nai B'rith

new_places_proposed.name: East Berlin
new_places_proposed.context: Communist section of Berlin where Hitler was buried; Lusia traveled there to see his grave

key_quotes.english: The sight of a flower that moved his heart delighted him. He saw countless horrors, but did not cry or scream.
I am not worried about my husband. He works outside the ghetto and is resourceful. But I must travel quickly to Shimon.
Do everything so that I won't be Jewish. Do everything so that I won't be Jewish.
You human monster, murderer like you, I, my husband and my children have remained alive while you lie here in a grave.
It is hard for me to believe that I am the Lusia who is telling this. Where did I find the spiritual strength?
Blessed am I to have achieved my dream.

overall_assessment: The memoir reveals Lusia's extraordinary resourcefulness and psychological resilience in surviving the Holocaust by successfully passing as a Polish woman while hiding her young son in a wooden box for over a year in Lwów under constant Nazi surveillance. The narrative confirms that David and Lusia were separated during much of the war (David in the Nadwórna ghetto until his escape; Lusia passing as 'Maria' in Lwów), and provides detailed accounts of their post-war journey through Brussels, Cyprus detention camps, and eventual resettlement in Israel. The memoir also establishes the critical role of non-Jewish helpers (Pietrozycki, Ivanov, the Hormak family, and Maria Ciccelik) in the family's survival, countering narratives that overlook gentile assistance during the Holocaust.
